data.title: US military leader meets with Rodríguez after Maduro ouster from Venezuela
data.description:
The U.S. military leader overseeing operations and forces in Latin America met with Venezuela’s interim President Delcy Rodríguez in Caracas on Wednesday, as the Trump administration continues to forge ties with the Venezuelan government after the ouster of Nicolas Maduro.  

Rodríguez huddled with U.S. Southern Command (Southcom) Commander Gen. Francis L. Donovan, Ambassador Laura F. Dogu — the U.S. top diplomat in Venezuela — and Joseph M. Humire, the acting assistant secretary of defense for Homeland Defense and the Americas. 

The discussion focused on security, steps to make sure President Trump’s three-phase plan for Venezuela, notably the stabilization of the nation, is being implemented and the importance of “shared security” across the Western Hemisphere, according to Southcom. 

Officials reiterated that Washington’s commitment to a “free, safe and prosperous Venezuela for the Venezuelan people, the United States, and the Western Hemisphere,” the military said in the press release. 

In a separate statement, Rodríguez’s press office said both sides agreed to work on forging a bilateral cooperation plan to fight drug trafficking in the region, along with migration and terrorism. 

The meeting highlighted that diplomacy should be the vehicle to settle differences and address problems of “binational and regional interest,” the press office said. 

The huddle comes a week after Energy Secretary Chris Wright met with Rodriguez in Caracas, when he assessed the state of the nation’s oil industry.

Last month, CIA Director John Ratcliffe also spoke with the interim leader, where he discussed the “potential opportunities for economic collaboration and that Venezuela can no longer be a safe haven for America’s adversaries, especially narcotraffickers.”

data.title: West Virginia alleges Apple failed to detect, report child sexual abuse material
data.description:
West Virginia in a new lawsuit is accusing Apple of knowingly permitting its iCloud platform to be used to store and distribute child sexual abuse material. 

The state’s attorney general, JB McCuskey, filed the complaint in the Circuit Court of Mason County on Thursday, demanding the company adopt “effective” detection measures for this illicit content. 

“Preserving the privacy of child predators is absolutely inexcusable. And more importantly, it violates West Virginia law,” McCuskey said in a press release. “Since Apple has so far refused to police themselves and do the morally right thing, I am filing this lawsuit to demand Apple follow the law, report these images, and stop re-victimizing children by allowing these images to be stored and shared.”

McCuskey cited court documents from a 2021 case involving the company that included a text message from the company’s anti-fraud executive, calling the software the “greatest platform for distributing child porn.” McCuskey alleged that, despite this admission, the company took “no meaningful action” to address this issue. 

Additionally, the attorney general pointed to discrepancies between Apple’s and other companies’ federally mandated reports of child sexual abuse material. 

“Apple’s failure to deploy available detection technology is not a passive oversight — it is a choice,” McCuskey said in the press release. 

The REPORT Act of 2024 expanded the types of child sexual exploitation that companies are required to report to the National Center for Missing and Exploited Children (NCMEC). 

Apple filed 250 reports of suspected child sexual exploitation on its platforms to the NCMEC’s tipline in 2024, compared to nearly 1.2 million reports filed separately by both Google and Snapchat that year. 

The attorney general’s office said that the case is the first of its kind against the company. 

In a Thursday statement shared with The Hill, Apple expressed its commitment to “protecting the safety and privacy of our users, especially children.”

“We are innovating every day to combat ever-evolving threats and maintain the safest, most trusted platform for kids,” the company said. 

“All of our industry-leading parental controls and features, like Communication Safety — which automatically intervenes on kids’ devices when nudity is detected in Messages, shared Photos, AirDrop and even live FaceTime calls — are designed with the safety, security, and privacy of our users at their core,” Apple continued.

Parents can activate the company’s Communication Safety feature on their children’s devices, which activates safeguards against sensitive content, including photos or videos containing nudity. This feature is turned on by default for users who indicate that they are under 18 years old. 

The company said it took further steps to incorporate parental controls for minors’ accounts in 2025, ensuring child safety settings are activated from when the child first begins using their Apple account. 

Apple has previously come under fire from child safety advocates for not intervening to prevent the spread of sexual material involving minors on iCloud. 

In a 2023 email responding to criticism from the Heat Initiative, Apple’s director of user privacy and child safety, Erik Neuenschwander, said “it was not practically possible” to scan personal data stored on iCloud without “imperiling the security and privacy of our users.” 

The lawsuit comes amid prevailing concerns over the spread of sexually explicit images of minors on social media and artificial intelligence platforms. Meta and OpenAI have faced criticism over the past year over claims that the companies failed to shield underage users of their chatbots from explicit content.

The West Virginia attorney general’s office said it is “seeking statutory and punitive damages” against the company in its Thursday lawsuit and demanded Apple implement “effective CSAM detection measures, and equitable remedies mandating safer product design going forward.”

data.title: Epstein fallout intensifies with former Prince Andrew's arrest
data.description:
12:30 Report is The Hill’s midday newsletter. Subscribe here.

Happy Thursday! It’s remarkable how warm 43 degrees and cloudy can feel after the frigid winter we’ve had. Think the groundhog wants to have a change of heart over this year’s prediction?

 

In today’s issue:

  • Lawmakers react to ex-Prince Andrew’s arrest

  • Fallout over leaders’ Epstein ties intensifies

  • Trump appoints Jared Kushner as peace envoy

  • Colbert, CBS controversy gives Talarico a boost

  • DC mayor seeks Trump’s help in historic sewage spill

  • Today’s big NASA test for the moon mission

 

👑 NEWS THIS MORNING

Andrew becomes first senior British royal arrested in nearly 400 years:

AP Photo | Kirsty Wigglesworth

Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor, formerly known as Prince Andrew, was arrested this morning over suspicions of misconduct while in public office. This comes amid new revelations around his relationship with the late convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.

What alleged misconduct?: The BBC reports that Mountbatten-Windsor has been accused of sharing confidential information with Epstein while serving as the U.K.’s special representative for trade and investment. However, the investigation is broader than just these revelations.

📸 Police activity at Andrew’s residence and former residence

What a way to spend your birthday: Today happens to be the former prince’s 66th birthday. 🎈

Andrew is the first British senior royal to be arrested since King Charles I in 1646.

King Charles III’s reaction: In a statement obtained by NewsNation, Charles says authorities have his “full and wholehearted support and co-operation” in the investigation into his brother, adding that “the law must take its course.”

A little reminder on Andrew’s past year: The king stripped his brother of his royal titles in October over his ties to Epstein.

💬 Follow today’s live blog

 

➤ HOW US LAWMAKERS ARE REACTING:

Numerous Democrats and some Republicans are pointing out that authorities in the United Kingdom have treated the Epstein files much more aggressively than in the U.S.

No charges have been brought in the United States against anyone other than Epstein or his former longtime associate Ghislaine Maxwell.

Democrats are also emphasizing a renewed desire to go after President Trump. As one House Democrat put it: “If a Prince can be held accountable, so can a President.”

Trump has not been accused of wrongdoing, despite his name appearing in the files, while Democrats assert the administration has not been transparent with information.

 

➤ MORE BACKLASH ON THE EPSTEIN SCANDAL:

Since the Epstein files release, a number of executives and former government figures have resigned from their positions over their alleged ties to the convicted sex offender.

Here’s a list of some of the VIPs so far:

❎ Kathy Ruemmler, a former Obama White House counsel, resigned as Goldman Sach’s top lawyer after the files revealed her close relationship with Epstein.

❎ Tom Pritzker, the cousin of Illinois Gov. JB Pritzker (D), resigned as executive chair of the Hyatt Hotels Corporation over his association with Epstein.

❎ Former United Kingdom Ambassador Peter Mandelson left his post last fall because of his alleged ties to Epstein. British Prime Minister Keir Starmer’s chief of staff Morgan McSweeney recently resigned over scrutiny for advising Starmer to appoint Mandelson — and so did Starmer’s communications director, Tim Allan, who is a friend of Mandelson.

❎ Brad Karp, the chair of the major law firm Paul, Weiss, resigned over email exchanges he allegedly had with Epstein.

❎ Sultan Ahmed bin Sulayem, the chair and CEO of DP World, resigned after he was named in the Epstein files.

❎ Norwegian Ambassador to Jordan Mona Juul resigned over her alleged ties to Epstein.

❎ The Slovakian prime minister’s national security adviser Miroslav Lajčák has resigned.

❎ Canadian physicist Lee Smolin is leaving the institute he helped start over his alleged ties to Epstein.

⚖️ Former Norwegian Prime Minister Thorbjørn Jagland has been charged with “gross corruption” over his ties to Epstein.

🎤 Oh, and Microsoft co-founder Bill Gates just canceled a high-profile AI speech amid the backlash over his past connection to Epstein.

 

☮️ HAPPENING TODAY

Peace be with the board:

Representatives from more than 40 countries are in Washington today for President Trump’s first meeting of his new Board of Peace. The group was meant to oversee the reconstruction of Gaza, but Trump says it should be involved in other conflicts around the world. Here are the highlights:

🔷 Trump pledges $10 billion: The president said the U.S. is pledging $10 billion to his Board of Peace. It’s unclear where exactly the money would be coming from for the pledge.

🔷 His son-in-law gets a new gig: Trump said he’s going to make his son-in-law Jared Kushner a special envoy for peace. Keep in mind that Kushner helped negotiate the Gaza ceasefire and has had a key unofficial role within the administration.

🔷 Trump teased Rubio: Trump joked that Secretary of State Marco Rubio did such a great job at the Munich Security Conference, he may fire him. “Marco, don’t do any better than you did please because if you do, you’re out of here,” Trump said. 🎥 Watch the clip

Back story on the group: Many of the U.S.’s allies have declined to join Trump’s group, in part because of Russia’s and Belarus’s involvement.

💻 Watch the meeting

 

🎙️ OTHER NEWS:

This interview is getting way more attention than it otherwise would have:

“Late Show” host Stephen Colbert says that CBS told him not to broadcast his interview with Texas Senate candidate James Talarico, citing pressure from the Trump administration’s Federal Communications Commission (FCC).

Colbert publicly bashed his network in a segment, which has been getting a lot of attention this week. Colbert then published the Talarico interview on YouTube.

💻 Watch Colbert’s Talarico interview on YouTube

💻 Watch Colbert’s segment bashing his network

For what it’s worth, CBS denies the accusation. CBS say Colbert was not prohibited from broadcasting the interview but was using caution to avoid triggering the FCC’s “equal time” rule. However, Colbert pushed back, arguing his talk show show is exempt from the rule that requires broadcast networks to provide equal air time to a candidate’s rivals.

But regardless, this controversy has given much more attention to both Talarico and the recent changes at CBS.

For example, Talarico is getting a boost: Colbert’s interview with Talarico has racked up more than 7.4 million views already. And his campaign has raised millions of dollars. Early voting has just kicked off in Texas — and this new attention may be a game changer for him, reports The Hill’s Julia Mueller.

Read more: ‘Talarico gets burst of momentum from CBS controversy’

Tidbit: FCC Chair Brendan Carr told reporters on Wednesday that he was “highly entertained” watching this saga unfold.

 

This should be fun when DC’s weather becomes swampy …:

Washington Mayor Muriel Bowser (D) declared a local public emergency over the massive sewage spill into the Potomac River. Bowser has requested federal support to help with the cleanup and sent a letter directly to President Trump, asking him to declare a major disaster in the D.C. area.

Backstory: the Potomac Interceptor collapsed on Jan. 19, spilling 243 million gallons of untreated wastewater into the river over the course of five days. DC Water then created a temporary fix.

A little, possibly problematic tidbit: Federal disaster relief support is currently not funded because of the ongoing Department of Homeland Security (DHS) shutdown.

Omg, this Politico headline on the sewage spill: “In feud with Wes Moore, Trump slings feces.”  

 

➤ SPEAKING OF THE DHS SHUTDOWN …:

Negotiations between the White House and Democratic leaders have hit a wall. Read Al Weaver’s reporting on where the discussions stand.

 

➤ QUICK HITS:

— The U.S. military is now in place for potential strikes against Iran. Trump has not hinted at his decision, but The New York Times reports he has the option starting as soon as this weekend.

— Trump’s daughter-in-law, Lara Trump, says the president has a prepared speech that details extraterrestrial life and spaceships. She says he’s waiting for the right time to deliver it. 👽

— A new approach may predict when Alzheimer’s symptoms begin in patients, The Washington Post’s Carolyn Y. Johnson reports.

— The Trump Organization has filed to trademark the use of President Trump’s name on airports, per The Associated Press.

— A federal judge threw out the ruling backing Trump’s policy for detaining migrants.

 

➤ MORE READS:

The Atlantic: The Disappointment of Young Trump Voters: Americans under 30 swung to the right in 2024, but they’re not getting what they voted for.

Politico: Look how much Canadians hate the United States now

The Wall Street Journal’s editorial board: You’re Paying 90% of Trump’s Tariffs

COMING UP

The House and Senate are out. President Trump is in Georgia this afternoon. (All times EST)

 

1:50 p.m. Trump visits a local business in Rome, Ga.

2:30 p.m. Trump participates in a podcast interview.

4:40 p.m. Trump tours a local factory.

4 p.m. Trump delivers remarks on the economy at an event in Rome. 💻 Livestream

Friday: The U.S. and Canadian men’s hockey teams play in separate Olympic semifinals. And Team USA and Team Canada are playing for gold today in the women’s hockey final this afternoon. 

🐝 INTERNET BUZZ

🌱 Celebrate: Today is National Chocolate Mint Day!

 

🚀 This is a big day for NASA: NASA will perform its big wet dress rehearsal today to determine whether its spacecraft is ready to fly. If all goes well, astronauts could perform their mission around the moon as soon as March 6 (!), per space.com. A simulated liftoff is expected at 8:30 p.m. EST. 💻 Watch it live

 

🍫🥜 The grandson of the Reese’s inventor is not happy: Brad Reese, whose grandfather invented the Reese’s Peanut Butter Cup in 1928, is criticizing the Hershey Co. for “quietly replacing” the chocolate and peanut butter used for the famous candy.

 

🫙Mayo *has to be* more controversial than this!: YouGov published a survey of Americans’ favorite and least favorite condiments. Peanut butter, honey and salsa had the most favorable views. Relish, fish sauce and hoisin sauce were at the bottom             . 📊 Americans’ views of 22 popular condiments

 

👋 AND FINALLY…

Did you know that volunteers at the Olympics have created a new sport? Let me have a turn!

 

 Stay Engaged 

I love hearing from you — send your feedback, tips and favorite cookie recipes to cmartel@thehill.com. Check out more newsletters here. See you next time!

data.title: Ricketts backs his wife, a Democrat, in Nebraska election
data.description:
Republican Sen. Pete Ricketts (Neb.) has endorsed his wife, Susanne Shore, who’s campaigning for a seat on the University of Nebraska Board of Regents as a Democrat.

The couple has traditionally dueled over which party’s candidate should rise to state leadership and both have frequently funded opposing campaigns for mayor and the Nebraska legislature. 

Despite conflicting political views on policy and partisan measures, Ricketts said his wife’s love for the Cornhusker State and its students is clear, describing her as someone who’d be “an outstanding voice for the university.”

“Her deep love for Nebraska is clear,” Ricketts told the Nebraska Examiner. 

“I usually vote straight Republican, but Susanne will be my one Democrat exception in 2026!” he added.

Shore said she’s learned a lot from watching her husband’s two successful gubernatorial campaigns and Senate win.

“He’s told me what it’s like to run for office, and I’ve seen it firsthand. It does feel different to be on this side, but so far it’s been amazing,” Shore told the Examiner.

She said Ricketts has been “nothing but supportive.”

The Hill has reached out to Ricketts’s office and Shore’s campaign for comment. 

Shore’s education experience started with a role as a resident assistant at her alma mater, Oklahoma State University.

Eventually, she moved on to work for the Dean of Students in Residential Life at the University of South Dakota. 

If elected to the NU Board of Regents, Shore said she would champion research, STEM studies and work to increase financial contributions from Congress in addition to private donors. 

If elected to a six-year term on the Board of Regents, Shore said she would help approve the budget and tuition rates alongside seven other eight regents representing individual districts.

data.title: A year of failure: Trump's attempt to end the Russia-Ukraine War
data.description:
President Trump returned to the White House in 2025 claiming he would quickly end the Russia-Ukraine war. But the sides today remain far apart on key issues while the war rages on.

Russian President Vladimir Putin has successfully strung Trump along to buy time to continue to fight. Trump’s bid to settle the conflict will fail unless he backs his diplomacy with pressure on Russia.

One year ago, Trump launched his effort to end the war. He got off to a bad start. In February 2025, he said Ukraine could not expect to recover all its territory or join NATO, a major tilt toward the Russian position before the warring sides had even agreed to engage. Then came the Oval Office ambush of Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky.

Trump produced a string of gifts for Putin. He broke ranks with Western leaders to say he would meet with the Russian leader and called for Russia’s return to the G8. U.S. officials opposed U.N. resolutions that fingered Russia as the aggressor state. Russia evaded new tariffs on “Tariff Liberation Day.” Most importantly for Moscow, Trump provided no new military or financial assistance to Ukraine, in contrast to the Biden administration’s large allocations. In return for all this, the United States received nothing, and the Kremlin did not move toward ending the war.

In March, Trump proposed a 30-day full ceasefire. Zelensky quickly agreed. Putin did not. Later in the spring, Trump fell into the habit of repeatedly giving Putin “two weeks” to adopt a more conciliatory attitude. The Kremlin repeatedly responded with silence or a “nyet.”

In the run-up to their August meeting in Alaska, Trump told journalists he would press Putin to accept a ceasefire and impose “severe consequences” if the Russian did not agree. Putin did not agree. Trump imposed no consequences.

To be sure, Trump has expressed frustration, even suggesting the Russian leader was “tapping me along.” But there has been little in the way of serious consequences. Putin appears to have figured the game out and understands that he can get away with a lot.

The pace on negotiations has intensified over the past few months. On one major issue, Zelensky has shown readiness to accept de facto (but not de jure) Russian occupation of territory on the Russian side of the line separating the two militaries. He has even offered to compromise on the portion of the Donbas region never occupied by the Russians. He understandably has linked territorial concessions — which will be painful for the Ukrainians — to solid security guarantees, to ensure Putin does not use a settlement to regenerate his military and attack again in a few years’ time.

Whereas Kyiv has evolved its negotiating stance, there is no evidence Moscow has altered its position. Russian demands seem little changed from what the Kremlin sought in 2022 or 2024. The Russians reject tangible security guarantees for Ukraine and insist a settlement must eliminate the war’s “root causes.” Moscow appears to regard a Ukraine that wishes to determine its own domestic and foreign policies as such a “root cause.”

Ignoring this reality, Trump recently said, “Russia wants to make a deal, and Zelensky is going to have to get moving.” Why should Zelensky move further when Putin has not moved at all?

By all appearances, the Russian leader believes he can attain his goals on the battlefield, even at great cost in Russian lives, and does not need to make concessions. If Trump wants a chance for his mediation bid to succeed, he has to back his diplomacy with pressure on the Kremlin and persuade Putin that he will fail on the battlefield and only face rising military and economic costs.

Trump has ready leverage … if he would use it. He could tighten sanctions on Russian oil exports to reduce the revenues that fuel Moscow’s war machine. He could have the U.S. government close loopholes that allow U.S. semiconductors and other hi tech products to reach Russia, where they find their way into Russian weapons.

Trump could also take steps to make clear to Putin that Ukraine will have the resources it needs to keep its military armed and in the fight. He could work with European countries to press Belgium for a solution that would allow use of frozen Russian Central Bank assets to back funding for Kyiv. If Trump wanted to send a particularly sharp message to Putin, he could ask Congress to approve $25 billion to $30 billion for U.S. military assistance to Ukraine.

These measures would raise pressure on the Russian economy, which already has entered a period of stagnation, while the promise of continuing funds for the Ukrainian military would force Putin to face the reality that more war will mean more Russian dead. And for what? The Russian army has made only marginal territorial gains the past three years.

Unfortunately, Trump has never appeared ready to put serious pressure on Putin. If Trump does not start doing so, his mediation effort will keep failing — a failure that he will own.

Steven Pifer, an affiliate with Stanford’s Center for International Security and Cooperation, is a former U.S. ambassador to Ukraine.

data.title: Former Prince Andrew arrested over Epstein ties: What to know
data.description:
Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor, who was recently stripped of his royal title due to his connections with the convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein, was arrested on Thursday morning, sparking the latest public controversy for the royal family. 

He was arrested at Sandringham House, a historical estate in the English countryside that belongs to the royal family. He was photographed leaving the 20,000-acre historical landmark in an unmarked car. 

The revered family residence was once home to his brother, King Charles III, grandfather, George VI and great-grandfather, George V.

Mountbatten-Windsor’s arrest follows months of controversy surrounding the former prince’s ties to Epstein after the release of emails documenting consistent communication between the two. 

Why was the former prince arrested? 

He was arrested for suspicion of misconduct in public office. While police did not immediately link his arrest to any past position or title, Mountbatten-Windsor did serve as the United Kingdom’s trade envoy between 2001-2011.

Reps. Ro Khanna (D-Calif.) and Thomas Massie (R-Ky.) said an email released by the Department of Justice that was recently unredacted shows the former prince allegedly shared state information with Epstein.

Trade envoys are bound by the same confidentiality clauses that apply to government ministers. On Thursday, Thames Valley police refused to name Mountbatten-Windsor as the individual arrested, citing national guidance but did share that they are conducting a search at addresses in Berkshire and Norfolk as a result of the arrest. 

“Following a thorough assessment, we have now opened an investigation into this allegation of misconduct in public office. It is important that we protect the integrity and objectivity of our investigation as we work with our partners to investigate this alleged offence,” Assistant Chief Constable Oliver Wright said in a statement obtained by BBC. 

“We understand the significant public interest in this case, and we will provide updates at the appropriate time.”

How has the royal family reacted?

The royal family was not forewarned about the Thursday arrest, according to a spokesman for King Charles, as reported by the New York Times.

“I have learned with the deepest concern the news about Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor and suspicion of misconduct in public office. What now follows is the full, fair and proper process by which this issue is investigated in the appropriate manner and by the appropriate authorities,” King Charles wrote in a statement signed “Charles R,” NBC News reported.

It bucks the traditional course of communication in which royals issue statements formally from Buckingham Palace.

What have Epstein survivors said about the former prince’s arrest?

The family of Virginia Roberts Giuffre issued a statement commending authorities for arresting the former prince. Giuffre said she was trafficked by Epstein and accused Mountbatten-Windsor of having sex with her in 2001. Former Prince Andrew’s arrest was not made in connection to Giuffre’s allegations. He has never been criminally charged for any alleged sex crimes.

Giuffre died by suicide last year after years of championing justice for sexual assault survivors.

“At last. Today, our broken hearts have been lifted at the news that no one is above the law, not even royalty,” Giuffre’s family wrote in a Thursday statement to The Hill.

“On behalf of our sister, Virginia Roberts Giuffre, we extend our gratitude to the UK’s Thames Valley Police for their investigation and arrest of Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor. He was never a prince,” it continues. 

The family added, “For survivors everywhere, Virginia did this for you.”

data.title: Chicago Bears, Indiana lawmakers take big step toward new stadium in Indiana
data.description:
INDIANAPOLIS — The Chicago Bears and Indiana state lawmakers took a big step Thursday morning toward the franchise moving across state lines to build a new stadium near Wolf Lake in Hammond, Indiana.

The Bears gave a statement signaling their “vision” to build a stadium in Hammond during a meeting by the Indiana House Ways and Means Committee, which unanimously passed a proposed amendment to Indiana Senate Bill 27 that names Hammond as the location for a new Bears stadium.

The Bears say the amendment’s passage by the full Indiana House of Representatives “would mark the most meaningful step forward in our stadium planning efforts to date.”

Indiana SB 27 was passed last month and establishes the Northwest Indiana Stadium Authority, but it does not discuss key financial details of what a Bears stadium would cost, such as who would pay for it and what taxes might be levied.

House Speaker Todd Huston (R-Fishers), who personally sponsored SB 27 in the House, filed the amendment Thursday that lays out the details of how Indiana will construct, finance and manage the new stadium.

The amendment passed out of committee by a 24-0 vote. Specifically, it details the creation of the Northwest Indiana Stadium Authority and the establishment of a Northwest Indiana Stadium Board, as well as allowing for the enactment of a Northwest Indiana Stadium Development District and Northwest Indiana Professional Sports Development Area to be located in Hammond.

Per the amendment, one of the Northwest Indiana Stadium Board’s powers would be “the issuance of bonds and notes to finance a capital improvement.”

“This is a once-in-a-generation opportunity,” Hammond Mayor Thomas McDermott Jr. told the House Ways and Means Committee, according to a news release issued by Hammond after the meeting.

“Hammond is ready to partner with the State of Indiana, the Chicago Bears, and will do whatever it takes to make this project a success and to welcome the Bears to their new home. This move by the State of Indiana and the Bears tells the rest of the country that Indiana is not just keeping up — it’s leading.”

Here is the full statement the Bears provided at Thursday’s meeting:

“The passage of SB 27 would mark the most meaningful step forward in our stadium planning efforts to date. We are committed to finishing the remaining site-specific necessary due diligence to support our vision to build a world-class stadium near the Wolf Lake area in Hammond, Indiana. We appreciate the leadership shown by Governor Braun, Speaker Huston, Senator Mishler and members of the Indiana General Assembly in establishing this critical framework and path forward to deliver a premier venue for all of Chicagoland and a destination for Bears fans and visitors from across the globe. We value our partnership and look forward to continuing to build our working relationship together.”

Indiana Gov. Mike Braun also issued a statement on his X feed.

“Indiana is open for business, and our pro-growth environment continues to attract major opportunities like this partnership with the Chicago Bears,” Gov. Braun wrote. “We’ve identified a promising site near Wolf Lake in Hammond and established a broad framework for negotiating a final deal. If approved, the proposed amendment to Senate Bill 27 puts forward the essential framework to complete this agreement, contingent upon site due diligence proceeding smoothly. The State of Indiana moves at the speed of business, and we’ve demonstrated that through our quick coordination between state agencies, local government, and the legislature to set the stage for a huge win for all Hoosiers. We have built a strong relationship with the Bears organization that will serve as the foundation for a public-private partnership, leading to the construction of a world-class stadium and a win for taxpayers.”

What about Arlington Heights?

Meanwhile, the latest news about a Bears stadium deal progressing in Hammond is sure to make waves among Illinois lawmakers and officials in northwest suburban Arlington Heights, where the Bears own the 326-acre Arlington Park property, formerly home to Arlington International Racecourse.

Business owners, elected leaders and residents say a new Bears stadium would be ideal for Arlington Heights, but there’s a standstill on the state level, as the Bears want a property tax deal that state legislators have still not agreed to.

The Illinois Revenue and Finance Committee had been scheduled to look at a bill on Thursday that would freeze the Bears’ property tax assessment there for up to 40 years and provide more than $850 million in infrastructure support.

However, that meeting was canceled. In a statement on X following Thursday’s developments in Indiana, a spokesperson for Gov. JB Pritzker said the Bears requested the meeting to be cancelled.

“Illinois was ready to move this bill forward,” the spokesperson said. “After a productive three-hour meeting (Wednesday), the Bears leaders requested the ILGA (General Assembly) pause the hearing to make further tweaks to the bill.

“This morning, we were surprised to see a statement lauding Indiana and ignoring Illinois.”

Nexstar’s WGN has reached out to Arlington Heights officials and is awaiting a reply. Meanwhile, in his State of the State address on Wednesday, Pritzker had said that progress was being made between the Bears and the state, and that talks would continue.

data.title: News, Fox Corp to match federal contribution to 'Trump accounts'
data.description:
Rupert Murdoch’s News and Fox Corps. will match the U.S. government’s one-time $1,000 contribution to the Section 530A accounts, informally known as “Trump Accounts” which the administration says are designed to “jumpstart the American Dream” for children across the country.

Starting in July, eligible U.S. employees of the two media properties “will have the opportunity to participate in the pilot program to receive a seed contribution of $1,000 from the U.S. Treasury into a specified account for every child born in the U.S. between January 1, 2025, and December 31, 2028,” the companies said on Thursday.

Both entities will make a one-time $1,000 supplemental contribution to these tax-advantaged investment accounts for employees who participate in the program.

News Corp. is the parent company of The Wall Street Journal, New York Post and other financial publications in the U.S. Fox Corp. is the parent company of Fox Broadcast Network and Fox News.

Established under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, signed by President Trump last year, the so-called “Trump accounts” are designed to set up a savings fund for children of American families to tap into for certain expenses once a child turns 18.

The funds are similar to a personal retirement account and will accept contributions from parents and their employers that are invested and grow until maturity.

Fox and News Corp. were founded by Murdoch, a conservative media mogul who President Trump has feuded with for years over coverage of him.

data.title: Substack, Polymarket announce partnership: 'Journalism is better when it's backed by live markets'
data.description:
Publishing platform Substack and prediction market Polymarket announced a new partnership on Wednesday.

The new partnership will allow Substack users to integrate Polymarket’s prediction data directly into their content.

Substack said one in five of Substack’s top 250 highest-revenue publications started using Polymarket’s data to produce content focusing on current events like election timelines, shifting AI expectations, debate around economic policy and speculation on awards shows like the Oscars.

“Prediction markets are an emerging technology that aggregates real-time estimates of what will happen in the future,” Substack co-founder Chris Best wrote on the platform.

“While the stock market lets people trade shares of companies, and the price reflects an estimate of how much a business is worth, Polymarket lets people trade shares of future events, like elections, economic trends, and scientific breakthroughs. The price reflects a market estimate of how likely an outcome is to happen.”

Polymarket, in a statement of its own said “journalism is better when it’s backed by live markets.”

The partnership comes as more states have questioned the legality of prediction markets, while Commodity Futures Trading Commission’s (CFTC) Chair Mike Selig has said he plans to fight against state laws and litigation meant to impose rules on prediction markets.

A growing number of journalists and media professionals have opted away from traditional news outlets and and media companies in favor of independent creator-driven platforms like Substack in recent years.

data.title: MAGA media cheers Quinn Hughes for praising U.S. after game-winning goal
data.description:
Minnesota Wild defenseman Quinn Hughes emerged as an overtime hero for Team USA on Wednesday, with a goal to lift the U.S. over Sweden en route to the men’s hockey semifinals at the Winter Olympics.

Hughes’s comments celebrating his home country are earning him praise from many across the conservative media ecosystem.

“I love the U.S., and it’s the greatest country in the world. So, I’m happy to represent here with these guys, and it was really special,” Hughes said after the game.

The comments were met with adulation from a wide array of political pundits, some of whom had decried other U.S. athletes competing in Milano-Cortina who had spoken less glowingly about the United States.

“Quinn Hughes scores winning OT goal says he loves representing the US because it’s the greatest country in the world. Finally, an Olympic athlete who loves the country,” wrote the right-wing sports and politics pundit Clay Travis on social media.

Fox News host Laura Ingraham, in a social media post of her own, called Hughes “a total superstar patriot!”

Trump ally and political commentator Monica Crowley praised Hughes for expressing “pure love for America,” on social media, writing “this is how it’s done.”

“Hockey Players >>>>> Freestyle Skiers,” wrote Grace Curley, who hosts a show on the conservative streamer Rumble. “… and it ain’t even close.”

The praise of Hughes stands in contrast to an onslaught of criticism many on the right, including President Trump, have leveled against other U.S. athletes who have expressed discomfort representing the U.S. given the policies of the Trump administration.

Freestyle skier Hunter Hess sparked a firestorm last week when he said he had “mixed feelings” about competing for the U.S., while figure skater Amber Glenn earned pushback from critics over what she said is the current climate for LGBTQ people under the Trump administration.

The U.S. hockey team will face off with Slovakia on Friday for a chance to meet in Sunday’s gold medal game against heavy favorite Canada, which plays Finland in its semifinal.

A potential U.S. vs. Canada final has been widely buzzed about since the beginning of the tournament and comes against a backdrop of increasing tensions between the two countries on issues like trade and the broader North American economy.
